commentaries.author: Ambrosiaster
commentaries.content: He exhorts them to pray for him in two ways: first that his thoughts may be filled by the Spirit for the full declaration of the mystery, and second that he may be given a faculty of bold utterance in proclaiming it. .

commentaries.author: Gaius Marius Victorinus
commentaries.content: Isn’t this wonderful? From those whom he himself has just now admonished, those whom he has instructed, to whom he has preached the gospel, he now asks for help. He is asking them for their prayers. He goes on to explain what he is asking them to pray for: “that utterance may be given to me.” His prayer is definite and specific, that a particular profit may accrue. –.

commentaries.author: Jerome
commentaries.content: This is to be understood as if he said, “Let the treasuries be opened. Let the promises hidden from ages be revealed. Let the Spirit enter to bring forth those things that have been concealed.” That this is indeed the meaning of this passage … is clarified by what follows: “in confidence,” he says, “to make known the mystery of the gospel.” .

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content: The Creator, when he displayed to the Church such constancy and plainness of speech in "making known the mystery of the gospel for which he was an ambassador in bonds "owing to his liberty in preaching-and actually requested (the Ephesians) to pray to God that this "open-mouthed utterance "might be continued to him?
